title: Why Version Control Matters
summary: Meet the three repositories used in this course, understand what Git records locally, and learn the inspect → change → verify habit you will use before typing any Git command.

sketchBlocks.title: A project needs a memory
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A clear, local project history answers questions a lone folder cannot: what changed, when, and why. This introduction explains prerequisites, the three repository journeys you will see across the course, the safe terminal workspace you'll use, and the simple habit you'll repeat whenever you touch project files.

Start role: you are a volunteer helping a community group build a small website. You have a folder of files on your computer — drafts, images, and notes. The course uses three separate repositories for three teaching goals:

- Guided (community-site): one repository that grows with step-by-step instruction so you learn each Git idea inside a single evolving project.
- Parallel module project (volunteer-hub): a different repository used across module projects to reapply learned workflows with less guidance.
- Capstone (neighborhood-guide): a fresh repository used only for the final capstone steps to prove independent transfer.

Prerequisites and safe workspace: you'll use the terminal-only runner inside the lesson workspace. You do not need network access, GitHub credentials, or package installs. When a hands-on exercise runs, hidden setup reconstructs the exact starting folder so your check focuses on the intended repository change, not accidental prior state.

The inspect → change → verify habit: before you run any Git command, follow three short steps: (1) inspect the current state with file and Git views, (2) make a small, focused change, and (3) verify the repository state shows only the intended change. This habit prevents accidental commits that mix unrelated work and makes history readable to collaborators and your future self.

Why we teach local history: saving a copy named site-final-2 or site-final-july leaves no recorded reason for differences and makes it hard to recover an intermediate idea. A recorded commit ties a message (the reason) to an exact snapshot of tracked files so you can compare, revert, or explain changes later. This course will show the same idea applied repeatedly until it becomes automatic.

sketchBlocks.title: Worked example: Recovering from an accidental overwrite
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: imagine the community-site folder contained five simple files on Day 1: index.html (homepage draft), about.md (project background), contact.md (contact details), styles.css (basic styles), and data.json (a small test dataset). You inspected the folder and decided these five files together were ready to record as a meaningful snapshot with a concise message like "Initial site structure".

Later the team copied a final-export folder into the same place and, by accident, replaced index.html with a flattened file missing some local edits. Looking only at the final-export copy on disk, it's hard to see what was lost: the new filename and a duplicate folder name tell no story about what changed or why. A set of folders named final, final-v2, or final-july quickly becomes ambiguous.

Because you recorded the earlier snapshot, the project history stores a named moment that captured the exact contents of the five tracked files and the short message describing your intent. Looking at that snapshot (the commit) answers the concrete question: what did the project contain at the moment I said "ready to record"? The recorded snapshot contains file contents as a coherent package, not just a list of filenames; it preserves the exact text of index.html and other tracked files at that moment.

Recovering from the overwrite is straightforward when you have a history: you inspect the history to find the commit message you wrote, open that snapshot to confirm it contains the expected content for index.html, and restore the tracked file from that snapshot. The repository history gives you a safe, explainable rewind point; duplicate final folders cannot provide the same context or selective restoration without guesswork.

What one snapshot/commit row means: a single commit entry in the history represents a complete recorded state of the tracked files at one moment plus the message you wrote to explain intent. If you see a history row labeled "Initial site structure," it means exactly the set of files you and your collaborators agreed were part of that meaningful milestone.

A common mistake this example prevents: committing many unrelated edits at once (for example, content, style, and data changes in one snapshot) mixes motivations. The inspect → change → verify habit keeps commits focused so each history row remains useful as a narrative of the project.

quizDraft.title: Main benefit of recorded commits
quizDraft.prompt: Which single statement best describes why recording commits locally is more useful than keeping several final-copy folders (like site-final, site-final-2)?
options: It uses less disk space than keeping multiple copies.
It stores snapshots with descriptive messages so changes and intent are clear.
It automatically deploys the site to a server.
It prevents anyone from editing files outside the repository.
quizDraft.hint: Think about what information a commit stores that a copied folder name does not.

help.concept: A commit records a snapshot of tracked files plus a message that explains the change; a copied folder lacks that structured history and explanation.
help.hint_1: A commit ties an exact set of file contents to a descriptive message and a point in project history.
help.hint_2: Compare: a folder named "final-july" only shows a copy without the reason it differs from other copies; a commit records both contents and context.

quizDraft.title: Setting a repository-local identity
quizDraft.prompt: Which command sets a repository-local user name so commits in that repository record the local identity?
quizDraft.hint: The question asks for a repository-local (not global) configuration setting.

help.concept: Use git config --local (or without --global) inside a repository to write a configuration value that applies only to that repository.
help.hint_1: Repository-local settings are stored in the repository's config and use --local when you want them confined to one repository.
help.hint_2: The command includes git config, the --local flag, the key user.name, and the quoted name value.

quizDraft.title: What Git records locally
quizDraft.prompt: Which of the following are recorded as part of the local repository state? (Select all that apply.)
options: The current working-tree file contents (what files look like right now)
Changes staged in the index waiting to become a commit
The repository's commit history of snapshots and messages
Local file modification timestamps only (OS-level times)
quizDraft.hint: Think about the three places you inspect: working tree, staging area, and commit history.

help.concept: Git's local repository state includes working-tree file contents, the staging area (what will be committed), and the commit history (recorded snapshots and messages). It does not record unrelated system-only metadata like arbitrary folder copies or external timestamps as a project history substitute.
help.hint_1: Consider what you see with git status (untracked/modified/staged) and git log (past commits).
help.hint_2: Choose the options that represent tracked snapshots, staged proposals, and the current working files — not external duplicate folders or system-only timestamps.

quizDraft.title: Inspect → Change → Verify sequence
quizDraft.prompt: Put the inspect → change → verify habit steps in the correct order for a focused Git workflow.
tokens: Make focused changes to the files
Inspect the current working tree and Git status
Verify history and that the working tree is clean
correctOrder: Inspect the current working tree and Git status
Make focused changes to the files
Verify history and that the working tree is clean
quizDraft.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

quizDraft.title: Initialize with main branch
quizDraft.prompt: Fill the blank: To create a new repository and set the initial branch to main, run: git init -b [blank1]
quizDraft.hint: Which branch name sets the primary branch to the modern default we use in this course?

help.concept: git init -b <name> initializes a repository and sets the initial branch name; the course uses the missing term as the default primary branch when initialization is part of the lesson.
help.hint_1: The initial branch commonly used now is named the missing term rather than master.
help.hint_2: Choose the branch name that the course requires when teaching initialization.

quizDraft.title: Where to list files to ignore
quizDraft.prompt: Fill the blank: A file that should never be tracked by Git (for example, temporary build output) belongs in [blank1]
quizDraft.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Pick the choice that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.
